card.indications: Major depressive disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, panic disorder, bulimia nervosa, and selected other psychiatric conditions.
card.mechanism: Selectively inhibits serotonin reuptake in the central nervous system, increasing serotonergic activity.
card.dosing: Common adult starting dose is 20 mg daily; lower starts may be used for panic disorder. Weekly delayed-release products are formulation specific. Dose changes should be gradual and individualized.
card.routeAdministration: Give in the morning if activating. May be taken with or without food. Do not crush delayed-release products. Allow an adequate trial because benefit develops over weeks.
card.contraindications: Concurrent or recent MAO inhibitor use, use with pimozide or thioridazine, and serious hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Boxed warning for suicidal thoughts and behaviors in children, adolescents, and young adults. Risks include serotonin syndrome, mania, hyponatremia, bleeding, seizures, angle-closure glaucoma, and long elimination half-life.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, insomnia, anxiety, headache, diarrhea, sweating, tremor, sexual dysfunction, and appetite or weight change.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Serotonin syndrome, severe hyponatremia, suicidal behavior, seizures, severe bleeding, mania, and serious skin or allergic reactions.
card.interactions: MAO inhibitors are contraindicated. Other serotonergic drugs increase serotonin syndrome risk. NSAIDs, antiplatelets, and anticoagulants increase bleeding. Fluoxetine inhibits CYP2D6 and can raise many drug levels.
card.monitoring: Monitor mood, suicide risk, anxiety, sleep, sodium in at-risk patients, bleeding, weight, serotonin syndrome, mania, and adherence.
card.patientTeaching: Take consistently and do not stop abruptly. Report worsening depression, suicidal thoughts, agitation, fever, confusion, muscle rigidity, severe headache, or unusual bleeding.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Avoid alcohol. Maintain hydration and regular meals if nausea or appetite changes occur.

card.indications: Maintenance treatment of asthma, allergic or nonallergic rhinitis, and selected inflammatory skin disorders depending on formulation.
card.mechanism: Activates glucocorticoid receptors and suppresses inflammatory mediators, edema, and immune responses.
card.dosing: Dose is formulation and indication specific. Inhaled and nasal products require regular daily use; topical products are applied in a thin layer for the shortest effective duration.
card.routeAdministration: Not a rescue bronchodilator. Rinse mouth after inhalation. Prime sprays as directed, aim nasal spray away from the septum, and use a spacer when appropriate. Avoid occlusive dressings unless prescribed.
card.contraindications: Primary treatment of status asthmaticus or acute bronchospasm, untreated local infection, or hypersensitivity to components. Some dry-powder products contain milk proteins.
card.warnings: Local candidiasis, adrenal suppression, reduced growth velocity, glaucoma, cataracts, osteoporosis, infection risk, paradoxical bronchospasm, epistaxis, and skin atrophy can occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Hoarseness, throat irritation, cough, oral candidiasis, headache, nasal irritation or bleeding, and local skin burning or dryness.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Adrenal crisis, severe bronchospasm, serious infection, glaucoma or cataracts, severe hypersensitivity, and significant skin atrophy with prolonged topical use.
card.interactions: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors such as ritonavir or ketoconazole can markedly increase systemic corticosteroid exposure.
card.monitoring: Monitor symptom control, rescue-inhaler use, oral or nasal mucosa, growth in children, eye symptoms, adrenal suppression, skin integrity, and infection.
card.patientTeaching: Use every day as prescribed, not for sudden breathing attacks. Rinse and spit after inhalation. Report white oral patches, worsening wheeze, vision changes, or persistent nosebleeds.
card.nutrition: No specific food restrictions. Adequate calcium, vitamin D, and protein support bone health during long-term high-dose therapy.

card.indications: Prevention and treatment of folate deficiency and megaloblastic anemia due to folate deficiency; supplementation during pregnancy and selected medication regimens.
card.mechanism: Converted to tetrahydrofolate cofactors required for DNA synthesis, red blood cell production, and amino-acid metabolism.
card.dosing: Common supplementation is 400 to 800 mcg daily; deficiency treatment often uses 1 mg daily. Higher doses may be prescribed for selected high-risk pregnancy or medication-related indications.
card.routeAdministration: May be taken with or without food. Confirm vitamin B12 status when treating megaloblastic anemia because folate can correct anemia while neurologic injury from B12 deficiency progresses.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. Folic acid alone should not be used to treat undiagnosed megaloblastic anemia until vitamin B12 deficiency is excluded.
card.warnings: Large doses can mask hematologic signs of vitamin B12 deficiency. Rare hypersensitivity reactions can occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Generally well tolerated; nausea, bloating, bitter taste, or mild rash may occur.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Anaphylaxis or severe allergic reaction is rare.
card.interactions: May reduce concentrations or effects of some anticonvulsants. Methotrexate and trimethoprim interfere with folate pathways; timing and form of folate supplementation must be clinician directed.
card.monitoring: Monitor CBC, reticulocyte response, folate and vitamin B12 status, neurologic symptoms, and adherence.
card.patientTeaching: Take as directed and do not substitute it for vitamin B12 treatment. Keep prenatal and laboratory appointments.
card.nutrition: Encourage folate-rich foods such as leafy greens, legumes, citrus, and fortified grains while maintaining the prescribed supplement.

card.indications: Adjunctive treatment of partial-onset seizures and treatment of postherpetic neuralgia; other neuropathic-pain uses are common when prescribed.
card.mechanism: Binds the alpha2-delta subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release.
card.dosing: Dose is titrated gradually and adjusted for renal function. Common immediate-release regimens are divided three times daily; extended-release products are not interchangeable.
card.routeAdministration: Give on a consistent schedule. Separate from aluminum- or magnesium-containing antacids by at least 2 hours. Taper gradually rather than stopping abruptly.
card.contraindications: Serious hypersensitivity. Use caution with renal impairment, respiratory disease, older age, substance-use risk, and concurrent opioids or sedatives.
card.warnings: Can cause serious respiratory depression, especially with opioids or lung disease; also suicidal thoughts, severe sedation, dizziness, edema, and withdrawal seizures.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dizziness, drowsiness, ataxia, fatigue, peripheral edema, blurred vision, and weight gain.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Respiratory depression, suicidal behavior, severe hypersensitivity, angioedema, status epilepticus after abrupt withdrawal, and severe falls or injury.
card.interactions: Opioids, alcohol, antihistamines, sleep drugs, and other CNS depressants increase sedation and respiratory risk. Antacids reduce absorption.
card.monitoring: Monitor pain or seizure control, renal function, sedation, respiratory status, gait, falls, edema, mood, and misuse.
card.patientTeaching: Do not stop suddenly. Avoid alcohol and unapproved sedatives. Do not drive until effects are known. Seek help for slowed breathing, swelling of face or throat, or suicidal thoughts.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Maintain hydration and monitor weight if edema or appetite changes occur.

card.indications: Treatment of serious susceptible gram-negative and selected gram-positive infections; topical and ophthalmic products treat local susceptible infections.
card.mechanism: Irreversibly binds the 30S bacterial ribosomal subunit, causing misreading of mRNA and inhibition of protein synthesis. It is bactericidal.
card.dosing: Systemic dosing is weight based and individualized by renal function, infection, and serum concentrations. Extended-interval or traditional divided dosing may be used.
card.routeAdministration: Obtain cultures before therapy when possible. Administer IV over the recommended time. Do not mix in the same solution with beta-lactam antibiotics. Ensure accurate weight and renal assessment.
card.contraindications: Serious hypersensitivity to gentamicin or other aminoglycosides. Use extreme caution with myasthenia gravis, renal impairment, hearing loss, and pregnancy.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include nephrotoxicity, irreversible ototoxicity, neuromuscular blockade, and fetal harm. Risk increases with prolonged therapy, high levels, dehydration, older age, and other nephrotoxic or ototoxic drugs.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Injection-site discomfort, nausea, headache, mild renal-function changes, and local irritation with topical products.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Acute kidney injury, permanent hearing loss, vestibular damage, respiratory paralysis, severe neuromuscular blockade, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: Loop diuretics, vancomycin, amphotericin B, cisplatin, contrast agents, and other nephrotoxic or ototoxic drugs increase toxicity. Neuromuscular blockers increase paralysis risk.
card.monitoring: Monitor peak and trough or other protocol-based levels, serum creatinine, urine output, hearing, balance, cultures, infection response, and neuromuscular weakness.
card.patientTeaching: Report decreased urine, swelling, ringing in the ears, hearing changes, dizziness, or muscle weakness immediately. Complete the prescribed course.
card.nutrition: Maintain adequate hydration unless restricted. No specific food restrictions apply.
